text:
Steals & Deals: Up to 88% off Jewelry, Skin Care, Omaha Steaks, More Gifts



Scott Stump

Swimmer Jessica Long had to dig deep to reach an "unimaginable" milestone by taking home the 30th Paralympic medal in her decorated career with an epic comeback.

She trailed for 300 meters of the women's 400-meter freestyle - S8 race in Paris on Sept. 4 before finding another gear in the final 100 meters to win what has become one of her signature events.

Long initially said after the delayed 2020 Tokyo Paralympics that she was not going to participate in the 400 again in Paris. She changed her mind and ended up bringing home her 17th career Paralympic gold medal.

"To come back three years later —and it’s been a really tough three years — to swim this race and to somehow get on the podium and win gold, it’s unimaginable," she said on TODAY on Sept. 5. "There’s so much work that went into it."

Long, 32, has now won the 400 freestyle - S8 race four times in her Paralympic career, having also captured the gold in 2004, 2008 and 2012.

"It feels unreal and heavy, very heavy," she said. "This one is so special to me. I definitely wanted to get to that 30 mark, and the gold is just so, so sweet."

The swimming star overtook Team Great Britain’s Alice Tai to win the race in 4:48.74 for a medal she won’t forget.

"I just knew that I had to put my head down and dig so deep," she said. "I remember at that final turn thinking to myself, 'Jess, do not even think about breathing.'

"I just knew I had it about 25 meters out. I knew if I put my head down I could reach for the wall first."

Long now has a chance at her 31st medal on Sept. 7 when she competes in the 100-meter butterfly - S8 race.

While her 30 medals are an incredible feat, she is not the most decorated U.S. Paralympian of all time. Swimmer Trischa Zorn-Hudson won a Paralympic record 55 medals, including 41 gold medals, over seven Paralympics, according to theU.S. Olympic & Paralympic Museum.

Long's remarkable Paralympic career began when she won three gold medals at the 2004 Paralympics in Athens at only 12 years old.

Her journey to stardom was the subject of aSuper Bowl commercialin 2021. She alsoshared her story of adoptionwith Jenna Bush Hager on TODAY that same year.

Long was adopted from a Siberian orphanage in Russia with a rare condition that required her legs to be amputated below the knee when she was 18 months old.

She's not done yet, either. Long said she is planning on swimming inthe 2028 Paralympics in Los Angelesbefore calling it a career.

"Oh my gosh absolutely, that is going to be my farewell where I say goodbye to the Paralympic movement," she said. "Paralympics No. 7 on home soil — I think it will be a dream."

Scott Stump is a trending reporter and the writer of the daily newsletter This is TODAY (which you shouldsubscribe to here!) that brings the day's news, health tips, parenting stories, recipes and uplifting stories right to your inbox. He has been a regular contributor for TODAY.com since 2011, producing features and news for pop culture, parents, politics, health, style, food and pretty much everything else.

title: US Paralympic Swimmer Jessica Long On 30th Paralympic Medal, Possible Retirement
description: Paralympic swimmer Jessica Long won her 30th medal at the 2024 Paris Paralympics. She talks on the TODAY show about having 17 golds and her future.
